name: Chin Chin
intro: I once heard someone describe chin chin as a crunchy biscuit that tastes like cake, and I couldn’t agree more. It’s very hard to stop eating once you start and once you give this recipe a try, you’ll understand why. In the traditional recipe, you’ll find eggs and milk, but this vegan take relies on plant-based milk to enrich the dough and is no less delicious! Here we will be making it the original way by frying, but chin chin can also be baked.

ingredients.item: 1/4 cup caster sugar 1/2 teaspoon cinnamon 1/2 teaspoon nutmeg Pinch of salt
3/4 cup soft brown sugar 1/4 cup tinned coconut milk

method: Place all the ingredients (except the sunflower oil) in a large mixing bowl and combine with your hands until you have a smooth, firm dough. If the dough feels too dry, add a little extra coconut milk. Be careful not to overwork or knead the dough. Cover the bowl and leave the dough to rest in the fridge for 10-15 minutes.

Heat some sunflower oil in a tall pot, enough for deep-frying. When hot, add the dough squares in batches and fry until golden brown all over, Remove from the oil using a slotted spoon and place on a tray lined with kitchen paper, to drain oﬀ any excess oil. The chin chin will harden and get crunchier as it cools down. Allow to cool completely before serving. Store in an airtight container for up to 2 weeks.
Add the sugar and coconut milk to a frying pan and cook over high heat, stirring often, until you have a thick syrup. This should take 5-6 minutes.

Add the salt and grated coconut and mix quickly to combine. Spread the mixture in the pan and leave to cook for 7-8 minutes, stirring occasionally, to allow excess moisture to evaporate. Spread the mixture on a tray lined with baking paper and leave to cool for 10-15 minutes, or until it is cool enough to handle.
